title: Using thermal mass and passive solar design to reduce heating load
subtitle: How to site glazing, manage internal mass, and avoid summer overheating in a retrofit context
excerpt: Thermal mass and passive solar design reduce heating demand by absorbing daytime solar gain and releasing it overnight. In a UK retrofit context the opportunities are limited but real: south-facing extensions, conservatory replacements, and garden room designs can incorporate mass cheaply. The risk of summer overheating rises with the same south glazing that delivers winter gain, so the design needs to address both.
sourceNotes: CIBSE Guide A Chapter 5 on thermal response of buildings. BRE Digest 454 on solar gain and overheating. Pat Borer and Cindy Harris, The Whole House Book (Centre for Alternative Technology). Tony Gwilliam, Low Energy Building Design Guidance. gov.uk Approved Document O (Overheating) 2021.

glossaryTerms.definition: The ability of a material to absorb, store, and release heat. High-mass materials like concrete, brick, and stone slow temperature swings. Low-mass materials like timber and plasterboard do not buffer heat storage significantly.
Heat entering a building through windows as solar radiation. In winter, south-facing glazing solar gain reduces heating demand. In summer, unshaded south and west glazing can overheat a room.
A measure of how much heat flows through one square metre of a building element per degree of temperature difference. Measured in W/m²·K.

content.text: Passive solar design does not require a purpose-built low-energy house to deliver benefit. A south-facing extension with a concrete or tile floor, a brick inner leaf, and 20 to 30 percent south glazing will admit enough winter
to reduce heating hours on sunny days. The floor and walls absorb the gain, then release it through the evening.

must be exposed to the room air, not buried under carpet or hidden behind board insulation. A 150 mm concrete slab or 215 mm brick wall facing the room works well. A 150 mm concrete slab under carpet does not. In a UK retrofit, existing brick party walls and solid external walls where you have not fitted internal insulation contribute mass. Fitted carpets everywhere eliminate the benefit of concrete floors.

The standard passive solar sizing rule for the UK is 20 to 30 percent of floor area as south glazing. Beyond 30 percent, summer overheating risk rises faster than winter heating savings. For a 15 m² extension that gives 3 to 4.5 m² of south glazing. Modern double glazing has a

of 1.2 to 1.4 W/m²·K. Triple glazing at 0.7 to 0.9 W/m²·K costs more but makes the west and north faces heat-loss-neutral in winter. The decision between double and triple depends on the overall fabric strategy.

South glazing that gives useful winter gain will overheat the room in June and July without shading. A horizontal overhang at window head height blocks mid-summer sun while letting in mid-winter sun. The geometry works because the sun sits much higher in summer (62 degrees at noon on 21 June in southern England) than in winter (15 degrees on 21 December). A 600 mm overhang over a 1.8 m high window blocks direct south sun from mid-April to mid-August. External blinds or shutters do the same job where an overhang cannot be built.
What works and what does not
Works: unglazed concrete or tile floor on a south extension with 20 to 25 percent glazing and a fixed overhang.
Works: internal brick wall retained in a south room when fitting internal insulation to other walls.
Does not work: carpeting an existing conservatory, adding more south glazing, and fitting no overhang or blind.
Does not work: relying on closing internal doors to manage overheating after the fact.

attrs.symptom: South room overheats in June but is cold in December

content.text: The glazing fraction is too high or the overhang is absent. Fit external blinds or a retractable awning for the summer period. Consider reducing the glazing area if replacing.
